coredata.dc:title: Development of fluorescence sensor and paper chips based on EDTA etched-CdTe@CdS QDs for the selective and sensitive detection of Cd(II) in rice samples
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Hui
Ding, Xiner
Chen, Zixin
Mao, Jialuo
Ma, Xiuna
Wei, Ziqi
Ye, Tai
Yuan, Min
Yu, Jinsong
Wu, Xiuxiu
Yin, Fengqin
Xu, Fei
coredata.dc:description: EDTA-etched CdTe@CdS QDs were used as the specific recognition element and signal probe of a fluorescence sensor or paper chip for the highly sensitive detection of Cd(II) with an electronic eye device as readout. The results showed that the CdTe@CdS QDs were successfully synthesized with a typical core–shell structure. Under the optimum conditions, the EDTA-etched CdTe@CdS QDs sensor achieved the sensitive detection of Cd(II) with a detection limit of 0.092 μM and a concentration range of 0.44–6.67 μM. The paper chips based on the EDTA-etched CdTe@CdS QDs sensor had a visual cut-off value of 0.44 μM with a short detection time (<15 min). The proposed method also exhibited excellent stability, high specificity, and good recovery for Cd(II) in real rice samples. These results suggested that the EDTA-etched CdTe@CdS QDs provide a promising platform for on-site and visual detection of Cd(II) in rice samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: EDTA etched-CdTe@CdS QDs
Cadmium ions
Detection
Fluorescence sensor
Paper chip

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of EDTA etched-CdTe@CdS QDs sensor
Preparation of paper chips based on EDTA etched-CdTe@CdS QDs sensor
Optimization of EDTA-etched CdTe@CdS QDs sensor
The detection limit of EDTA-etched CdTe/CdS QDs sensor
Evaluation of paper chips based on EDTA-etched CdTe@CdS QDs sensor
Detection of Cd(Ⅱ) in rice samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shanghai Municipality

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Shanghai ( 23ZR1443400 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The analysis process of EDTA etched-CdTe@CdS QDs sensor toward Cd(II) with electronic eye device as readout (a). The preparation and analysis process of paper-based chip based on EDTA etched-CdTe@CdS QDs sensor toward Cd(II) with electronic eye device as readout (b). The detection mechanism of Cd(II) based on CdTe@CdS QDs etching technology (c).
Fluorescence spectra (a), TEM image (b), FT-IR spectra (c), XRD patterns (d) and high-resolution S2p spectra (e) of CdTe@CdS QDs.

Effect of etching time (a), fluorescence recovery time (b), and EDTA concentration (c) on the fluorescence recovery of EDTA etched-CdTe@CdS QDs; The calibration curve (d) of EDTA etched-CdTe@CdS QDs sensor for Cd(II) detection.

(a) FT-IR spectra of fiberglass paper and APTES-modified paper chip; (b) The cut-off value of EDTA etched-CdTe@CdS QDs chips for Cd(Ⅱ) detection in the rice sample with the concentration of Cd(Ⅱ) at 0 μg/L (A), 10 μg/L (B), 50 μg/L (C), 100 μg/L (D), 500 μg/L (E), and 1000 μg/L (F); (c) The selectivity experiment of the EDTA etched-CdTe@CdS QDs chips for Cd(Ⅱ) detection with the concentration of Fe(III), Hg(II), K(I), Mg(II), Mn(II), Na(I) at 5 mg/L and the concentration of Cd(Ⅱ) at 0.1 mg/L; (d) Stability test of the EDTA etched-CdTe@CdS QDs chips.
Recoveries of Cd(Ⅱ) in three kinds of rice samples.

head.ce:title: Development of fluorescence sensor and paper chips based on EDTA etched-CdTe@CdS QDs for the selective and sensitive detection of Cd(II) in rice samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Health Science and Engineering, Shanghai Engineering Research Center of Food Rapid Detection, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, P.O. Box 454, No. 516, Jungong Road, Shanghai 200093, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Health Science and Engineering
Shanghai Engineering Research Center of Food Rapid Detection
University of Shanghai for Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Health Science and Engineering, Shanghai Engineering Research Center of Food Rapid Detection, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, P.O. Box 454, No. 516, Jungong Road, Shanghai 200093, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: EDTA-etched CdTe@CdS QDs were used as the specific recognition element and signal probe of a fluorescence sensor or paper chip for the highly sensitive detection of Cd(II) with an electronic eye device as readout. The results showed that the CdTe@CdS QDs were successfully synthesized with a typical core–shell structure. Under the optimum conditions, the EDTA-etched CdTe@CdS QDs sensor achieved the sensitive detection of Cd(II) with a detection limit of 0.092 μM and a concentration range of 0.44–6.67 μM. The paper chips based on the EDTA-etched CdTe@CdS QDs sensor had a visual cut-off value of 0.44 μM with a short detection time (<15 min). The proposed method also exhibited excellent stability, high specificity, and good recovery for Cd(II) in real rice samples. These results suggested that the EDTA-etched CdTe@CdS QDs provide a promising platform for on-site and visual detection of Cd(II) in rice samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With economic development, cadmium has become widely utilized in batteries, fossil fuels, chemical fertilizers, plastics, and other applications. The release of cadmium ions into the environment can cause severe air, water, and soil pollution [1,2]. When cadmium [Cd(II)] accumulates in grain, it not only damages the grain yield and quality but also can be enriched in the human body through the food chain causing severe human health impacts, especially in Asia [3,4]. Cd(II) has been classified as a Group 1 carcinogen by the World Health Organization's International Agency [5]. Therefore, it is necessary to detect Cd(II) in grain for food safety and environmental monitoring.

Many conventional analytical methods have been used to detect Cd(II), such as electrochemical stripping analysis [6], atomic absorption spectrometry [7], and atomic fluorescence spectrometry [8]. Although these methods have high sensitivity and specificity, their application is limited due to their high cost, cumbersome operations, and longer analysis periods [9,10]. Rapid detection methods based on recognition elements such as aptamers [11] and imprinted polymers [12] have been developed to detect Cd(II) in different food matrices. To achieve quantitative and sensitive detections novel nanoparticles—such as up-conversion phosphorescence materials [13], quantum dots [14], and florescent microspheres [15]—are often used as labels in these methods. For example, Luan et al. (2016) established a highly selective method for detecting Cd(II) based on a specific aptasensor with an unmodified double-stranded deoxyribonucleic acid–specific dye [16]. The linear range of the proposed method was 0.10–100 µg/mL with a detection limit of 0.038 ng/mL. Bakhshpour designed an ion-imprinted surface plasmon resonance sensor for the real-time sensing of Cd(II), with the detection limit was 0.01 µg/L [17]. However, these methods are unsuitable for the rapid and reliable on-site determination of Cd(II) as their synthetic and detection procedures are complex [18]. Therefore, the development of a rapid, sensitive, and visual technology for the on-site detection of Cd(II) would be of enormous value.

Quantum dots (QDs), a fluorescent semiconductor nanomaterial, have been widely used in the detection of Cd(II) due to their outstanding characteristics such as wide and adjustable excitation wavelength, narrow and symmetric emission wavelength, high fluorescence yield, high stability, and good luminescence performance. Recently, various QD-based fluorescence sensors [19,20] have been developed for the detection of Cd(II) based on the luminescence enhancement (turn on) or quenching (turn off) mode resulting from the direct interactions of Cd(II) and QDs. These interactions include electrostatic interactions [14], fluorescence resonance energy transfer [21], the static quenching effect [22], and chelate-enhanced fluorescence quenching or enhancement of QDs. However, these interactions of QDs with Cd(II) are insufficiently specific and many species in the sample solution can also induce changes in the fluorescence intensity of QDs.

QDs etching technology is a method of selectively etching the surface of QDs by chemical method and has advantages like strong selectivity and simple operation [23,24]. In the etching process, a metal chelating agent, like EDTA, binds with metal ions on the QD surface to partly break the QD structure. This not only induces the pronounced photoluminescence quenching of the QDs but also forms many recognition sites that are complementary to metal ions on the surface of the QDs [25]. Therefore, EDTA-etched QDs can act as both a specific recognition element and a response signal probe. Wang et al. (2018) used EDTA as a metal chelating agent to etch Cd(II) onto the surface of CdTe/CdS QDs for the detection of Cd(II) [26]. The linear response range of the etched QDs for Cd(II) was 0.05–9 μM with a detection limit of 0.032 μM. However, such a method often requires a commercially available spectrofluorometer to measure the fluorescent intensity of QDs. Although the fluorescence spectrophotometer has high detection sensitivity, it is not suitable for on-site detection because of its inconvenient handling and high cost. Therefore, it is highly desirable to develop a portable and low-cost method that can precisely measure the fluorescence intensity for realizing the on-site analysis.

In this study, CdTe/CdS QDs etched by EDTA were prepared as the specific recognition element and signal probe of Cd(II). Subsequently, the CdTe/CdS QDs sensor and paper chip based on EDTA-etched CdTe/CdS QDs sensor were developed for the on-site and visual detection of Cd(II) in rice samples using an electronic eye device as readout.

Cadmium chloride anhydrous (CdCl2), sodium tellurite (Na2TeO3), thioglycolic acid (TGA), thioacetamide, and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co. (Shanghai, China). Sodium borohydride (NaBH4) was purchased from Sigma Chemical Co. Glass fiber paper was obtained from Blue-Sky Environmental Testing Supplies Co. (Shandong, China). All other chemicals were of reagent grade or higher, which were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

Briefly, 26.25 mM TGA and 15 mM CdCl2 were dissolved in 38 mL ultrapure water with the molar ratio of TGA/Cd at 2:1. The pH of the mixture was adjusted to 10 using NaOH solution (1 M) and deaerated with N2 for 15 min to synthesize the Cd-precursor. Then, with magnetic stirring, 10 mg NaBH4 and 15 mg Na2TeO3 were added to the Cd-precursor solution. The reaction solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 200 °C in the oven for 30 min to obtain the CdTe QDs. In the following, 3.0 mM thioacetamide was dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water and added to as-prepared CdTe QDs under N2 bubbling and stirring constantly. The CdTe@CdS QDs were synthesized by further aging the CdTe cluster solution at 80 °C for 32 min. The synthesized CdTe/CdS QDs were purified by adding isopropyl alcohol, centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min, and washed four times. Finally, the CdTe/CdS QDs of 0.33 μM were dispersed in PBS buffer solution at pH 7.4 and stored at 4 °C for further use.

The size and morphology of CdTe/CdS QDs were investigated using a JEM2100 transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analyzer. The Fourier infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) of CdTe/CdS QDs was recorded using a Thermo Scientific NicoletiS20 (USA). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of CdTe/CdS QDs were analyzed by a Panalytical Empyrean (The Netherlands). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of CdTe/CdS QDs was analyzed by an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, USA) with an Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV). The luminescence spectra of the CdTe/CdS QDs were recorded with a RF-6000 152 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Company, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of EDTA etched-CdTe@CdS QDs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: A fluorescence sensor based on CdTe/CdS QDs etching technology was designed to detect Cd(II) with an electronic eye device as readout. The electronic eye mainly consists of three parts: a smartphone, a 600 μL sample slot, two UV lamps with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, and a light-proof cassette with a camera hole at the top for taking photos. The analysis process of Cd(II) using this fluorescence sensor is shown in Fig. 1 a. First, 200 μL of 0.33 μM CdTe@CdS QDs and 200 μL of 10 mM EDTA solution were mixed in a 2 mL test tube and incubated for 4 min at room temperature to etch the Cd(II) on the surface of CdTe@CdS QD and quench their fluorescence. Afterward, 600 μL of Tris-HCl (16 mM, pH = 8) and 200 μL different concentrations of Cd(II) solution were added to the tube and mixed thoroughly. The mixture was incubated for 9 min at room temperature and then placed in the sample slot of the electronic eye device. Subsequently, the fluorescence photos of samples were recorded using a digital camera under UV light excitation. The obtained photos were further analyzed by Image J software to achieve their fluorescence value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of paper chips based on EDTA etched-CdTe@CdS QDs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: A portable paper chip based on the CdTe@CdS QDs sensor was further designed for the on-site and visual detection of Cd(II) [27]. The schematic diagram for paper chip fabrication and detection using an electronic eye device as readout is shown in Fig. 1b. The fiberglass papers (5 cm × 5 cm) were firstly activated in mesylate (50 %, v/v) solution for 7 h with the shaker. The activated fiberglass paper was further soaked in a 3 mL of ethanol solution (70 %) containing 150 μL of APTES and 3 % of NH3·H2O (v/v) to prepare an APTES-modified paper chip. After incubation for 24 h at room temperature, the modified paper chip was overnight dried at 60 °C in a vacuum oven. Following this, CdTe@CdS QDs were immobilized on the surface of the modified paper chip via an EDC-NHS coupling reaction. Briefly, the modified paper chip was soaked in 10 mL of PB buffer solution (0.01 M, pH 6.0) containing 10 μg EDC, 20 μg NHS, and 20 μL of CdTe@CdS QDs, and then shaken at 25 °C for 2 h. The obtained paper chips were washed thrice using a PBS buffer (0.01 M, pH 7.4) to remove unbound CdTe@CdS QDs and dried in a vacuum oven. Then, it was stored at 4 °C in a dark environment until use.

In the detection process of Cd(II), the paper chips were soaked in 3 mL of EDTA solution (500 μM) and incubated at room temperature for 5 min to complete the etching step. Then, the etched paper chips were placed in the sample slot of the electronic eye device and measured according to the method described in 2.2.

The crushed rice samples were spiked with a Cd(II) concentration of 20–100 µg/L and extracted using the dual-frequency ultrasonic-assisted enzyme digestion process according to the method reported by Cao et al. [28]. Briefly, 0.5 g spiked rice sample, 0.2 mL α –amylase, and 0.4 g trypsin were mixed in a 10 mL centrifuge tube and vortexed for 30 s. Then, MES buffer solution (0.1 M, pH 6.0) of 2 mL as extraction solvent was added to the above centrifuge tube and sonicated for 6 min at 65 °C under a dual-frequency ultrasonic mode with a 40 kHz ultrasonic bath (KQ-400KDE, Kunshan Ultrasonic Instruments Co., Ltd., Kunshan, China) and a 20 kHz ultrasonic probe (JY92-IIDN, Ningbo Science Biotechnology Co., Ltd., Ningbo, China). The obtained mixture was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 3 min to obtain supernatant. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm nylon membrane filter and stored at 4 °C.

In the synthesis of CdTe@CdS QDs, TGA is usually selected as a stabilizing agent to control the growth and agglomeration of QDs by affecting their nucleation [29]. The Cd-thiol complex formed between the sulfhydryl group of TGA and cadmium ions can be used as a passivation layer to eliminate surface defects of CdTe@CdS QDs and effectively enhance their fluorescence signal. EDTA, a metal chelating agent, can partly break the Cd-thiol passivation layer by combining with Cd(II) on CdTe@CdS QDs, resulting in Cd(II) defects on the surface of QDs. The partial loss of the Cd-thiol complex not only induces the pronounced photoluminescence quenching of CdTe@CdS QDs but also produces specific sites for Cd(II) recognition [30]. Consequently, with the introduction of Cd(II) in the sample solution, the Cd(II) recombines with the ligand TGA to repair the defects on the surface of these EDTA-etched CdTe@CdS QDs by forming Cd-thiol complexes, which results in fluorescence restoration. Therefore, the EDTA-etched CdTe@CdS QDs can simultaneously act as a specific recognition element and response signal probe in the detection of Cd(II). The “turn on” detection mode based on the CdTe@CdS QD etching technology is shown in Fig. 1c.

The TGA-capped CdTe/CdS QDs were prepared by the in-situ deposition of the CdS shell on the CdTe core. The optical properties of the obtained CdTe/CdS QDs were investigated by observing the shift in their emission wavelength at different synthesis times. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the typical emission peak appeared and continuously red-shifted from 550 to 640 nm with prolonged synthesis time, indicating that the size of CdTe/CdS QDs increased consistently due to the growth of the CdS shell [31]. The redshift also showed that the synthesized QDs had a core–shell structure rather than a CdTexS1–x alloy structure since the formation of CdTexS1–x-alloyed QDs would lead to a blue shift in the photoluminescence spectra [32]. Furthermore, the photoluminescence emission intensity of the CdTe/CdS QDs was significantly enhanced with prolonged synthesis time. This was because the growth of the CdS shell effectively eliminated the surface defects of the CdTe core. When the synthesis time was 32 min, the maximal yield (21.4 %) of the CdTe/CdS QDs was achieved with a full width at half-maximum (fwhm) of 61 nm.

The size and morphology of the CdTe/CdS QDs were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2b, the CdTe@CdS QDs exhibited a near-spherical shape with a uniform size of 2.26 nm (inset). The surface chemical groups of the CdTe@CdS QDs were analyzed by FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 2c, the characteristic absorption band at ∼ 3415 cm−1 belongs to the stretching of the hydroxyl group [33]. The peak at ∼ 2925 cm−1 is attributed to the CH2 vibrations of the TGA, indicating the presence of TGA molecules that are coated on the surface of the QDs. The strong absorption at 1557 cm−1 and a relatively symmetrical vibration at 1396 cm−1 imply that the carboxyl groups are present on the surface of QDs and turned into their anion, COO− [34]. The disappearance of the thiols group (S–H) vibration at 2550 cm−1 and the appearance of the Cd–S bond at 664 cm−1 suggests that the cleavage of the S-H bond and the formation of a new Cd-S bond between TGA and cadmium ions on the surface of QDs [35].

Fig. 2d shows the XRD patterns of the CdTe QDs and CdTe/CdS QDs at the range of 5°-90°. The diffraction peaks of CdTe QDs are located at 25.2°, 42.0°, and 48.1° corresponding to the (111), (220), and (311) crystal planes of zinc blende cubic phases [36]. While the CdTe/CdS QDs pattern shifts slightly to the higher angles and the shape of the diffraction peaks are nearly the same as that of CdTe QDs, which indicates the epitaxial growth of the CdS shell over the CdTe QDs [37]. It further confirms the formation of the CdTe/CdS core–shell structure rather than the CdxTe1−xSy alloy structure. The XPS spectra of the CdTe QDs and the CdTe/CdS QDs are shown in Fig. 2e. The high-resolution S2p spectra of the CdTe QDs exhibited two typical energy peaks at 162.1 eV (S2p3/2) and 163.52 eV (S2P1/2), corresponding to the Cd-SR bonds arising from the coordination of thiols and cadmium ions on the surface of the CdTe QDs [24]. After growing a CdS shell on the surface of the CdTe QDs, the binding energy of the CdTe@CdS QDs was shifted to 161.87 eV for S2p3/2 and 163.02 eV for S2p1/2, indicating that the coordination of S in the CdTe@CdS QDs was different to that in Cd-SR. The lower binding energy of S2p mainly corresponded to the S from CdS, which further confirmed the synthesis of CdTe@CdS QDs with a core–shell structure [38]. The presence of S in the interior and on the surface of the CdTe@CdS QDs provided surface passivation and controlled the surface defects, improving their stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of EDTA-etched CdTe@CdS QDs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence sensor based on EDTA-etched CdTe/CdS QDs was designed to detect Cd(II) with an electronic eye device as readout. The effect of the etching time (1–6 min) on the fluorescence recovery (ΔF) was investigated in this study. The ΔF was obtained from the difference between the fluorescence intensity of the CdTe@CdS QDs in the presence of Cd(II) and the fluorescence intensity of the CdTe@CdS QDs after etching. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the ΔF value of the CdTe@CdS QDs gradually increased with increasing etching time. This was because the shorter etching time could not induce enough Cd(II) recognition cavities on the surface of the CdTe@CdS QDs, which affected the sensitivity of the proposed method [39]. When the etching time was 4 min, the maximal ΔF value of 111.98 was obtained, suggesting that QDs could be etched efficiently by EDTA in 4 min. However, when the etching time was more than 4 min, the ΔF decreased rapidly. This phenomenon could be because excessive etching of EDTA would accelerate the oxidation of Te2– and damage the CdTe/CdS QD structure, which reduces fluorescence recovery efficiency [40]. Therefore, an etching time of 4 min was chosen in this study.

The effect of the recovery time (2–9 min) on the ΔF was further investigated in this study. Fig. 3b shows that as the recovery time increased, the ΔF value increased gradually and peaked (ΔF = 114.56) at 8 min, indicating that cadmium ions filled the defects on the CdTe@CdS QDs surface rapidly in 8 min [41]. Therefore, the optimal fluorescence recovery time was chosen as 8 min. The concentration of EDTA was optimized to obtain a good detection sensitivity for the EDTA-etched CdTe/CdS QDs sensor. Fig. 3c shows that increasing the concentration of EDTA resulted in a gradual increase in the ΔF value. The maximal fluorescence recovery (ΔF = 136.48) was obtained at an EDTA concentration of 10 μM. However, when the concentration of EDTA was further increased up to 50 μM, the ΔF decreased rapidly from 136.48 to 15. This phenomenon could be explained by the fact that, at a low EDTA concentration, the surface of the CdTe@CdS QDs was etched with only a few cavities; therefore, there were not enough Cd(II) recognition sites on the surface of the QDs. However, at a high EDTA concentration, the competitive chelation between excess EDTA with Cd(II) prevents the Cd(II) from entering the cavities on the surface of the QDs and results in a reduction in fluorescence recovery [24]. Therefore, the concentration of EDTA was set at 10 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection limit of EDTA-etched CdTe/CdS QDs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The ΔF value of the EDTA-etched CdTe/CdS QDs sensor at different concentrations of Cd(II) was measured to achieve the quantitative detection of Cd(II). As shown in Fig. 3d, under optimal conditions, the increase in the ΔF values could be apparently observed with the increasing Cd(II) concentration. The calibration curve is further constructed by plotting ΔF value against Cd(II) concentrations. The linear regression equation could be expressed as Y = 140.6 + 0.08132X (R2 = 0.98) in the range from 0.44 μM to 6.67 μM. The LOD was calculated as 0.092 μM (LOD = 3 σ/K, where 3 is the signal-to-noise ratio, σ is the standard deviation of blank measurements, and K is the slope of the working curve). According to Table S1, the LOD of the EDTA-etched CdTe/CdS QDs sensor for Cd(II) detection was better than that of most QDs-based methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of paper chips based on EDTA-etched CdTe@CdS QDs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The FT-IR spectra of fiberglass paper and an APTES-modified paper chip are shown in Fig. 4 a. The peaks of the fiberglass paper at 980 cm−1 and 780 cm−1 corresponded to the antisymmetric vibration stretching and oscillating vibration absorptions of Si-O-Si, respectively, which are characteristic absorption peaks of silica [42]. The peak at 1600 cm−1 was assigned to the bending vibration absorption of H-O-H. Compared with the fiberglass paper, the characteristic peaks of the APTES-modified paper chip at 1380, 1340, 1560, and 1300 cm−1 were assigned to the C-H bending vibration, C-N stretching vibration, N-H denaturation vibration, and H-O bending vibration, respectively. The wide absorption peak of the APTES-modified paper chip at 2997 cm−1 corresponded to the N-H stretching vibration of APTES [43], proving that APTES was successfully grafted onto the fiberglass paper. The CdTe@CdS QDs were then immobilized on the surface of the APTES-modified paper chip via an EDC-NHS coupling reaction (Fig. 4b, control). The resulting paper chips maintained fluorescence values of greater than 95 % after washing 20 times with water, indicating that the CdTe@CdS QDs had been firmly grafted onto the paper chip.

The cut-off values of the developed CdTe@CdS QDs chip were determined in rice samples with the addition of different concentrations of Cd(Ⅱ). The cut-off value is defined as the minimum concentration that produces a distinct fluorescence difference relative to the blank, as determined by the naked eye. As shown in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence intensity of the CdTe@CdS QDs chip after etching was the lowest. As the Cd(Ⅱ) concentration increased, the fluorescence intensity of the CdTe@CdS QDs chip was gradually enhanced. When the Cd(Ⅱ) concentration was 50 μg/L, the fluorescence intensity of the CdTe@CdS QDs chip was significantly higher than that at 0 and 20 μg/L and could be clearly distinguished by the naked eye. Therefore, the visual detection limit of the CdTe@CdS QDs chip for the qualitative determination of Cd(Ⅱ) was 50 μg/L (0.44 μM). The maximum residue limits of Cd(II) in rice from different countries/areas are listed in Table S2. The above results showed that the sensitivity of the reported CdTe@CdS QDs chip for Cd(II) could meet the detection requirements.

The selectivity of the proposed methods was investigated using different competing ions [Fe(III), Hg(II), K(I), Mg(II), Mn(II), and Na(I)]. As shown in Fig. 4c, the presence of Fe(III), Mn(II), and Mg(II) at high concentrations (5000 μg/L) resulted in only slight fluorescence recovery of the paper chip, while Cd(Ⅱ) (50 μg/L) resulted in more than 50 % fluorescence recovery of the paper chip. These results suggested that Cd(Ⅱ) recognition sites on the CdTe@CdS QDs surface were successfully created during the etching process. Therefore, the EDTA-etched CdTe@CdS QD chips displayed high selectivity toward Cd(Ⅱ).

The stability of the CdTe@CdS QDs chips for Cd(Ⅱ) detection (50, 100, 200 μg/L) was determined via batch-to-batch (n = 5) and within-batch (n = 3) experiments. Fig. 4d shows that the paper chips based on EDTA-etched CdTe/CdS QDs sensor have good stability and are very suitable for the on-site and visual detection of Cd(Ⅱ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cd(Ⅱ) in rice samples
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of the developed CdTe@CdS QDs sensor and paper chip based on EDTA-etched CdTe/CdS QDs sensor was determined by analyzing spiked rice samples. As shown in Table 1 , recoveries of Cd(Ⅱ) in the spiked rice ranged from 76.2 to 104.2 %, with RSD values of 4.80–11.40 %. According to the European Union SANTE guideline [44], the average recovery should be 70–120 %, with an RSD of less than or equal to 20 %. Therefore, the developed fluorescence sensor and the CdTe@CdS QDs chip met the requirements for the on-site and visual detection of Cd(Ⅱ) in actual samples.

In this study, the CdTe/CdS QDs sensor and paper chip based on EDTA-etched CdTe/CdS QDs sensor were developed for the on-site and visual detection of Cd(II) in rice samples using an electronic eye device as readout. Under the optimal conditions, the EDTA-etched CdTe@CdS QDs sensor achieved the sensitive detection of Cd(II) with a detection limit of 0.092 μM. The paper chips based on the EDTA-etched CdTe@CdS QDs sensor had a cut-off value of 0.44 μM for the visual detection of Cd(II). The proposed methods also exhibited excellent stability, high specificity, and good recovery for Cd(II) in real rice samples. These results suggested that the developed EDTA-etched CdTe/CdS QDs sensor and EDTA-etched CdTe@CdS QDs chip could serve as alternative tools for the on-site and visual detection of Cd(II) in actual samples.
Ethical approval
This article does not contain any studies with human participants or animals performed by any of the authors.

Hui Cao: . Xiner Ding: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation. Zixin Chen: Data curation. Jialuo Mao: Methodology. Xiuna Ma: Visualization. Ziqi Wei: Formal analysis. Tai Ye: Investigation. Min Yuan: Software. Jinsong Yu: Resources. Xiuxiu Wu: Formal analysis. Fengqin Yin: Supervision. Fei Xu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Shanghai (23ZR1443400).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109734.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
